Author: Anjana Sarkar
Title: Beyond the Chatbox: Engineering a Financial-Grade Agentic Loop
Text:
Beyond the Chatbox: Engineering a Financial-Grade Agentic Loop
From intent classification to LLM-as-a-Judge: What matters when we build production grade financial agents that prioritizes safety over “smartness.”
Introduction
When building for finance, the stakes are binary: either your system is accurate and compliant, or it is a liability. The “AI” part of an AI application is often the smallest component. The real work lies in the architecture of trust.
In this article, I’ll walk through the thinking behind moving a financial chat agent from a fragile prototype to a robust, agentic system capable of handling complex queries while maintaining strict guardrails. And if you would like to follow along with some code, you’ll find it here. https://github.com/anjanas/finance-chat-agent
1. Intent Classification: The Router Pattern
In a financial context, not all questions are created equal. A “Yes/No” query about a transaction status requires a very different execution path than a request for a “detailed explanation of tax-loss harvesting.”
Here is where where using LLM as a High-Level Router comes in. By classifying intent upfront, we can swap system prompts dynamically. This ensures that the model doesn’t “over-think” a numerical answer or “under-explain” a complex strategy.
Practitioner Insight: Don’t use a 400B parameter model for routing. A well-prompted small model (SLM) can handle classification with sub-50ms latency, saving you both cost and compute.
2. Input Guardrails: Defining the “No-Fly Zone”
Financial applications are prime targets for prompt injections and “drift” (users asking about the weather or political history). You need to implement strict Input Guardrails to enforce domain specificity.
If a query doesn’t pass our “Financial Relevance” filter, the agent politely declines. This isn’t just about user experience; it’s about security. Preventing the model from engaging in irrelevant topics minimizes the surface area for social engineering and hallucinations.
3. The Output Formatter: UX as a First-Class Citizen
In finance, a decimal point in the wrong place is a P0 incident. We decoupled the “Reasoning” from the “Presentation.” Our Output Formatter ensures that regardless of how the model “thinks,” the final numbers are delivered in a consistent, human-readable format (e.g., standardizing currency, percentages, and table layouts).
In the following screenshot, you can see how a large number needs to get formatted into a readable number — 119.58B
4. The Agentic Loop and “LLM-as-a-Judge”
The most significant shift in architecture that is evolving in 2026 is moving from a linear pipeline to an Agentic Loop.
For complex queries, we don’t take the first answer. We introduce a Judge Agent. If the Judge identifies a logic error or a missing data point, it sends the response back to the “Producer” agent with specific feedback for a second pass.
- The Constraint: We set a strict “Iteration Cap” (usually 3) to prevent infinite loops and runaway latency.
5. Evaluation: Metrics That Matter
You cannot manage what you cannot measure. A rigorous Evaluation Framework is a must.
- Evaluation Data: In this example, you’ll find an ”Evaluation Set” of Easy, Medium, and Hard questions. A reliable agent is one that can accurately answer the easy as well as the hard questions. The evaluation data becomes a golden dataset to measure against while you experiment with different models, prompts or changes in architecture.
- Key Metrics:
The key metrics you want to measure are
- Token Efficiency: How much are we spending per successful resolution?
- P99 Latency: Are we getting constant latency or there are situations where answer is taking too long? This will point to areas in your architecture or infrastructure that needs to get better. This might also point to optimizations in the agentic loop.
- Correctness (Judge-Verified): What is our “Pass@1” vs “Pass@10” rate?
- Consistency: Are the answers for Pass@10 wildly different?
- Robustness: Is incorrect data throwing it off? How resilient is it against errors in external API calls?
Conclusion: The Takeaway
Building a financial agent isn’t about finding the “best” model; it’s about building a system that can handle the model’s inherent non-determinism. By focusing on intent, guardrails, and iterative evaluation, you can build a financial agentic application that your compliance team — and your users — can actually trust.
